are an Australian professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football club, representing both the [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") and [St George](/wiki/St_George,_Sydney "St George, Sydney") regions of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). The club has competed in the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") (NRL) since [1999](/wiki/NRL_season_1999 "NRL season 1999") after a joint-venture was formed between [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")'s [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") (est. 1921) and [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong")'s [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") (est. 1982).[[1]](#cite_note-1) The club officially formed as the game's first joint-venture club on 23 September 1998 and remains the only inter-city team in the NRL.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

(Redirected from [St George Illawarra Dragons](/w/index.php?title=St_George_Illawarra_Dragons&redirect=no "St George Illawarra Dragons"))

Not to be confused with [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") or [Saint George (disambiguation)](/wiki/Saint_George_(disambiguation) "Saint George (disambiguation)").

The **St. George Illawarra Dragons** are an Australian professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football club, representing both the [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") and [St George](/wiki/St_George,_Sydney "St George, Sydney") regions of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). The club has competed in the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") (NRL) since [1999](/wiki/NRL_season_1999 "NRL season 1999") after a joint-venture was formed between [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")'s [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") (est. 1921) and [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong")'s [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") (est. 1982).[[1]](#cite_note-1) The club officially formed as the game's first joint-venture club on 23 September 1998 and remains the only inter-city team in the NRL.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The team has leagues clubs in both [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong") and the Sydney suburb of [Kogarah](/wiki/Kogarah,_New_South_Wales "Kogarah, New South Wales"), plays games regularly at both [WIN Stadium](/wiki/Wollongong_Showground "Wollongong Showground") in Wollongong and [Jubilee Oval](/wiki/Jubilee_Oval "Jubilee Oval") in Kogarah, and has its high performance training and administration headquarters at The [Bruce Gordon](/wiki/Bruce_Gordon_(businessman) "Bruce Gordon (businessman)") Centre in the Wollongong suburb of [Fairy Meadow](/wiki/Fairy_Meadow,_New_South_Wales "Fairy Meadow, New South Wales"). From 1999 to 2006 the club was jointly owned by the St. George Dragons 50% and Illawarra Steelers 50%. In 2006 [WIN Corporation](/wiki/WIN_Corporation "WIN Corporation") purchased 50% of the Illawarra Steelers stake in the club before purchasing the rest of the Illawarra Steelers' share in August 2018.

The Dragons reached the [grand final](/wiki/Grand_final "Grand final") in their first season in [1999](/wiki/1999_NRL_season "1999 NRL season"), losing to the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"). St. George Illawarra is one of only two clubs (the other being the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") in 1908) to finish runner-up in its inaugural season. St. George Illawarra also fields teams in local competitions within the [St. George](/wiki/St_George,_New_South_Wales "St George, New South Wales") and [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") regions. In 2010, the Dragons won their second successive minor premiership and became the first team to be awarded the [J. J. Giltinan Shield](/wiki/J._J._Giltinan_Shield "J. J. Giltinan Shield") in consecutive years since the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") was formed in 1998.

The Dragons won the [2010 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2010_NRL_Grand_Final "2010 NRL Grand Final") against the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") 32–8 at [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia"), [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"). They then won the [2011 World Club Challenge](/wiki/2011_World_Club_Challenge "2011 World Club Challenge"), defeating the [Wigan Warriors](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") 21–15 at [DW Stadium](/wiki/DW_Stadium "DW Stadium") in the UK. With its [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge") victory, the club became the first Australian team since the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") in 1992–3 to win the minor premiership, premiership and World Club Challenge simultaneously. As one club, St. George Illawarra Dragons have won 16 NRL premierships. This makes them the third highest premiership winning team in the NRL competition, behind South Sydney Rabbitohs and Sydney Roosters.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Performance_Chart_NRL_SGI.svg/250px-Performance_Chart_NRL_SGI.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Performance_Chart_NRL_SGI.svg)

Chart of yearly table positions for St. George Illawarra Dragons in First Grade [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL")

See also: [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") and [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers")

In the wake of the [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war") of the mid-1990s, and the resulting [split competition of 1997](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League_season_1997 "Australian Rugby League season 1997"), the [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") found themselves struggling financially and seemed unlikely to survive past 1999. The [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") largely financed a proposal for a joint venture which would see the St George Illawarra Dragons playing in both Kogarah and Wollongong. Essentially, St George would provide the money while Illawarra would provide a broader junior and fan base. With the NRL's intention to rationalise the competition from 20 teams down to 14 teams and with a $4M incentive and a relaxing of salary cap requirements for a joint venture, the Dragons and the Steelers ensured their survival by forming the League's first joint venture on 23 September 1998.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### David Waite and Andrew Farrar (1999–2002)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: David Waite and Andrew Farrar (1999–2002)")]

A joint venture entity being a new concept in Australian rugby league, the public watched closely in anticipation of success or failure. No-one was certain how the top players sourced from the two clubs would perform when they ran out onto the field. They were unsuccessful in their first outing, losing to the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") 20–10, but by the 4th round they had started to form some cohesion and would go on to achieve a top eight position on the competition ladder by the end of the regular season. In the semi-finals the Dragons won against the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") at Olympic Park, before returning to Sydney to dispose of the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") and the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") to reach the Grand Final.

In the 1999 Grand Final, the club were leading by 14–0 at the break in front of a world record crowd of 107,999. A fairytale of a title in their first year as a joint venture seemed destined to come true; something of an advertisement to any other clubs considering the option of a joint venture, but [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") recovered from their poor start and went into the final minutes of the game with St. George Illawarra leading by 18–14. The Dragons were forced to perform a drop kick from their own goal line, and on the fifth tackle [Brett Kimmorley](/wiki/Brett_Kimmorley "Brett Kimmorley") kicked high towards St. George Illawarra's corner of the field. As Melbourne's winger Craig Smith caught the ball over the goal-line he was knocked unconscious in a tackle by [Jamie Ainscough](/wiki/Jamie_Ainscough "Jamie Ainscough") and lost the ball. Referee [Bill Harrigan](/wiki/Bill_Harrigan "Bill Harrigan") deferred to the [video referee](/wiki/Video_referee "Video referee") who ruled a penalty try on the grounds that contact had been made with the head, giving Melbourne a 20–18 lead and the premiership title.

In the [2000 NRL season](/wiki/2000_NRL_season "2000 NRL season") though they had a rough start, and in Round 5, St. George Illawarra recorded their worst defeat for the club, losing 70–10 against the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"). This was the first game these two teams played against each other since the 1999 grand final, and was played at the [MCG](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground"). On 3 May to the surprise and shock of fans and club officials, [Anthony Mundine](/wiki/Anthony_Mundine "Anthony Mundine") announced his retirement from the club and rugby league in general with immediate effect to pursue a career in [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"). The Dragons were not going well in the first half of the 2000 season, and the main coach [David Waite](/wiki/David_Waite "David Waite") was replaced with assistant coach, [Andrew Farrar](/wiki/Andrew_Farrar "Andrew Farrar"). Soon after the replacement of coaches, St. George Illawarra recorded their best ever win. They defeated the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") 54–0 in round 14 at WIN Stadium, with debutant [Amos Roberts](/wiki/Amos_Roberts "Amos Roberts") scoring a record 22 points (one try and nine goals) for any first grade player on debut. St. George Illawarra, however, missed out of the finals series, and came 9th. Despite their poor season, [Trent Barrett](/wiki/Trent_Barrett "Trent Barrett") was awarded with the [Dally M Medal](/wiki/Dally_M_Medal "Dally M Medal").

### Nathan Brown (2003–2008)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Nathan Brown (2003–2008)")]

In 2003, Nathan Brown, the joint venture's first number 9 achieved the position of coach, becoming the youngest non-playing coach in premiership history at the age of 29. St. George Illawarra finished the season 10th. Nathan Brown was also involved in a sideline incident when he had slapped [Trent Barrett](/wiki/Trent_Barrett "Trent Barrett") in Wollongong during a game against Manly, and was issued a $5000 fine.

2004 saw the final season of the try scoring talent [Nathan Blacklock](/wiki/Nathan_Blacklock "Nathan Blacklock"). An extraordinary scoreline was seen in the round 25 clash with Manly. Trailing 34–10 after 53 minutes the club came back to win the match 36–34. This was the second biggest comeback in Australian Rugby League history. Finishing 5th, the club were eliminated in the first round of the finals after losing in a thrilling game 30–31 to 4th placed Penrith.

In 2005, after their worst start to a season yet (losing 5 of their first 6 games), the St. George Illawarra side finished second in a close season on the ladder at completion of the [2005 NRL season](/wiki/2005_NRL_season "2005 NRL season"), just behind minor premiers the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") on points difference. After progressing to the finals they defeated local rivals the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") 28–22 in the quarter-final of the finals series though the Dragons eventually fell 12–20 to eventual champions the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") in the Preliminary Final. The club broke the ground record at [WIN Stadium](/wiki/WIN_Stadium "WIN Stadium") twice in the 2005 season. First against rivals the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") and then at their home quarter final game against the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks").

In early 2006, WIN Television Network bought a 25% stake in the [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") Club for $6.5 million, erasing most of the debt Illawarra had to St. George. This formalised the strong support the network has shown for the Steelers in years gone by and ensured that Wollongong continued to host world class rugby league matches.

After a bad start to the [2006 NRL season](/wiki/2006_NRL_season "2006 NRL season"), the club put on a midseason seven/game winning streak. This was followed by a form slump, with a record five consecutive losses, only to return to form with a victory over the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"), and continue this positive form leading into the finals. The club ended the regular season in sixth position.

In the finals campaign, St George Illawarra faced the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") at [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Suncorp_Stadium "Suncorp Stadium") in the first round, St. George Illawarra won the game 20–4. In the second round, they met the [Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles") at the [Sydney Football Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(1988) "Sydney Football Stadium (1988)") winning 28–0. In the grand final qualifier, they played the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), where the club went down 24–10 bowing out of season 2006.

St. George Illawarra could take solace from the fact that they did defeat the eventual premiers, Brisbane, three times during the 2006 season – 26–12 at Wollongong in Round 4, 18–16 at Suncorp Stadium in Round 15 and the aforementioned qualifying final match.

Season 2007 saw an early injury to [Mark Gasnier](/wiki/Mark_Gasnier "Mark Gasnier") in the [Charity Shield](/wiki/Rugby_League_Charity_Shield_(Australia) "Rugby League Charity Shield (Australia)") match against [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") creating another poor start for the Dragons. Combined with the loss of key players such as [Luke Bailey](/wiki/Luke_Bailey_(rugby_league) "Luke Bailey (rugby league)"), [Trent Barrett](/wiki/Trent_Barrett "Trent Barrett") and [Shaun Timmins](/wiki/Shaun_Timmins "Shaun Timmins") in the off-season, the Dragons faced NRL newcomers, the [Gold Coast Titans](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans "Gold Coast Titans"), in Round 1. The joint-venture club won the historic match 20–18, however lost 6 more matches after that leaving the Saints sitting in 15th place on the NRL Ladder. This was a very inexperienced Dragons team that seemed destined for a poor injury plagued season. However, in Round 17 (vs [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") at [WIN Stadium](/wiki/WIN_Stadium "WIN Stadium")), the Saints scored four times more points than their previous game average, winning the match 58–16, equalling their highest ever score and their largest victory in three years. The club's season ended with a 28–24 loss to the [Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles") at Brookvale Oval, finishing in 13th place, their lowest finish to date.

2008 saw the end of an era, with coach [Nathan Brown](/wiki/Nathan_Brown_(rugby_league_born_1973) "Nathan Brown (rugby league born 1973)")'s contract not renewed after yet another poor start to the season, winning one of their first six matches. The club received a boost in May when former dual-code international player [Wendell Sailor](/wiki/Wendell_Sailor "Wendell Sailor") joined the team. Several notable players departed at the end of the year including [Jason Ryles](/wiki/Jason_Ryles "Jason Ryles")[[4]](#cite_note-4) and captain [Mark Gasnier](/wiki/Mark_Gasnier "Mark Gasnier"). The club finished the season in 7th place, however they lost in the first round of the finals to eventual premiers [Manly](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles") (whom they defeated 20–18 in round 11) 34–6 at [Brookvale Oval](/wiki/Brookvale_Oval "Brookvale Oval") ending yet another disappointing season. The era under Brown was one of frustration for Dragons fans, as there were high expectations for the strong side, but ultimately no premierships.

### Wayne Bennett (2009–2011)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Wayne Bennett (2009–2011)")]

2009, a new season a new coach and ground sponsor WIN at Jubilee Oval. Several new faces joined the club, including Jeremy Smith, Darius Boyd, Neville Costigan, Michael Weyman and Luke Priddis. The season opener against the Storm ended in a thrilling golden point extra time loss (17–16). Round 3 set a new crowd record with the victorious return to WIN Jubilee against Cronulla.

They were consistently placed first on the competition ladder in 2009, contributed by a continuous winning streak of seven games since their win against the [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") on 14 June. They lost first position after a three-game losing streak while the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") won all games in that period, but remain in the top three. However, in the final round of the 2009 regular season, St. George Illawarra defeated in-form [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") 37–0, a game in which winger Brett Morris obtained a hat-trick and man of the match honours, while Canterbury lost 34–12 to the Wests Tigers. In the same game, St. George Illawarra five-eighth [Jamie Soward](/wiki/Jamie_Soward "Jamie Soward") surpassed [Harry Bath](/wiki/Harry_Bath "Harry Bath")'s record for most points in a season by a St. George player. As a result, St. George Illawarra won the minor premiership for the 2009 and Wayne Bennett's first season at the club, but the team was eliminated from the finals after losing both their finals matches, therefore becoming the first minor premiers since 1993. In the first match they were humbled in front of a packed Jubilee Stadium, before traveling to face Bennett's former team. Brisbane defeated the Saints.

Season 2010 saw the club lose seven matches – the same tally as in the 2009 home-and-away season, against eventual wooden spooners the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), 2008 premiers the [Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles"), their perennial bogey team the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders"), 2003 premiers the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") in round 17, the [Gold Coast Titans](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans "Gold Coast Titans") in extra time in round 20, Bennett's old team the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") in round 21 and a second time to the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") in round 24. The team has been placed first since round five.

The team won its second minor premiership in succession (the first NRL team to do so since the inception of the NRL in 1998) with victory against the Newcastle Knights in the penultimate round of the [2010 NRL season](/wiki/2010_NRL_season "2010 NRL season"). The joint-venture club had led the competition from round five right through to the final round, spending 21 consecutive weeks on top of the ladder and therefore spending the longest period of time on top of the ladder in NRL history (not including the Melbourne Storm).

St George Illawarra met Manly in the first week of the 2010 finals and produced a 28–0 victory. St. George Illawarra then defeated [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") 13–12 with [Jamie Soward](/wiki/Jamie_Soward "Jamie Soward") kicking the winning field goal for a place in the [2010 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2010_NRL_Grand_Final "2010 NRL Grand Final").

In the [2010 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2010_NRL_Grand_Final "2010 NRL Grand Final"), St. George Illawarra defeated the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") 32–8, to secure the club's first grand final victory. The club overcame a poor first half performance to pull a thrilling victory out of the hat. This victory made Wayne Bennett's grand final record at 7 wins from 8 and thus continuing Brian Smith's poor grand final record of 4 losses from 4. [Darius Boyd](/wiki/Darius_Boyd "Darius Boyd") won a premiership with Bennett and the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") in 2006, won the Clive Churchill Medal for best on ground in the match. The Grand Final victory also ended 31 years of hurt for St. George fans as the club had lost previous finals in 1985, 1992, 1993, 1996 and 1999.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

St George Illawarra began their 2011 Season with pre-season wins in trials over Canterbury and South Sydney in the Charity Shield. The club also claimed its first [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge") Championship, defeating [Wigan Warriors](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") 21–15 at [DW Stadium](/wiki/DW_Stadium "DW Stadium"). As of Round 14 2011, the Dragons have won 10 straight (excluding a 14–14 draw against Parramatta in Round 13).

It was announced in March 2011 that Bennett would leave St. George Illawarra after the [2011 NRL season](/wiki/2011_NRL_season "2011 NRL season"), signing a four-year deal with the [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") from the 2012 season onwards. Steve Price was named as his successor.

### Steve Price and Paul McGregor (2012 – 2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Steve Price and Paul McGregor (2012 – 2020)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d4/Dragons_cheerleaders.jpg/250px-Dragons_cheerleaders.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dragons_cheerleaders.jpg)

The club's cheer squad, The Flames, performing during the Dragons' Anzac Day match against the Roosters in 2018.

2012 saw the club with a new coach. St. George Illawarra narrowly missed out on a Finals spot, finishing up in 9th position. Several notable players departed from the club at the end of the season, including [Ben Hornby](/wiki/Ben_Hornby "Ben Hornby") and [Dean Young](/wiki/Dean_Young_(rugby_league) "Dean Young (rugby league)"), who both retired from the sport. The start of the [2013 NRL season](/wiki/2013_NRL_season "2013 NRL season") saw the arrival of few key acquisitions, namely Gerard Beale from the Broncos and [Tyson Frizell](/wiki/Tyson_Frizell "Tyson Frizell") from the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks").

The season was plagued by inconsistent form, alongside injuries and speculation regarding Price's future at the club. After winning three straight matches on the lead up to the annual Anzac Day clash with the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"), it was announced that Price would be signed for another year with an option for a second at the clubs disposal. Despite the clubs faith in Price as well as the arrival of star fullback [Josh Dugan](/wiki/Josh_Dugan "Josh Dugan") from Canberra – in a mid-season transfer, the club was unable to string together consistent performances and finished the year at 14th position.

Star five-eighth [Gareth Widdop](/wiki/Gareth_Widdop "Gareth Widdop") joined the club for 2014, but by late May 2014, just after the star signing of [Benji Marshall](/wiki/Benji_Marshall "Benji Marshall"), the board sacked Price as coach due to some embarrassing defeats to start the season. Assistant coach [Paul McGregor](/wiki/Paul_McGregor_(rugby_league) "Paul McGregor (rugby league)") was named as his replacement until the end of the season, and was eventually employed full-time following improved results. This culminated in the club returning to the finals in [2015](/wiki/2015_NRL_season "2015 NRL season") and enjoying a successful year.

In the [2017 NRL season](/wiki/2017_NRL_season "2017 NRL season"), St George Illawarra lead the competition after the first eight rounds before a dramatic slide which saw the club record only six wins for the rest of the season. In the final match of the regular season, St George Illawarra played against Canterbury with a win guaranteeing them a finals spot and jumping ahead of North Queensland. With under ten minutes to play, St George Illawarra were ahead on the scoreboard before two quick tries gave Canterbury a 26–20 victory.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The [2018 NRL season](/wiki/2018_NRL_season "2018 NRL season") saw the arrival of two star signings, English international [James Graham](/wiki/James_Graham_(rugby_league) "James Graham (rugby league)")[[9]](#cite_note-9) and the [Australian](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") and Queensland halfback [Ben Hunt](/wiki/Ben_Hunt_(rugby_league) "Ben Hunt (rugby league)").[[10]](#cite_note-10) These signings and a successful season culminated in the club returning to the finals in [2018](/wiki/2018_NRL_season "2018 NRL season") and enjoyed a successful year. On 9 September 2018, St. George Illawarra Dragons won their first qualifying final in 8 years, defeating the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") 48–18 at [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The following week, St George Illawarra played against South Sydney in week 2 of the finals series and were defeated 13–12 in a tight game. The match was also the first time since 1984 that the two clubs had played each other in the finals.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The [Intrust Super Premiership NSW](/wiki/Intrust_Super_Premiership_NSW "Intrust Super Premiership NSW") St. George Illawarra also had a strong year finishing second at the end of the regular season were they made the preliminary final but were defeated 28–26 by Canterbury.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In August 2018 WIN Corporation purchased the [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") remaining 25% stake in the club for a "commercially in confidence" sum. The St George Dragons and WIN Corporation now have a 50% stake each. WIN Corporation has paid off the $6 million debt the St. George Illawarra Dragons owed the NRL. The club's home venues will continue to be WIN Stadium and Jubilee Stadium.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Before the start of the [2019 NRL season](/wiki/2019_NRL_season "2019 NRL season"), St. George Illawarra were expected by many to reach the finals and continue their good form from the previous year. A few weeks out from the club's first match, [Jack de Belin](/wiki/Jack_de_Belin "Jack de Belin") was stood down indefinitely by the [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") in relation to an incident which happened in December 2018. On 18 April 2019, McGregor was granted a two-year contract extension by St. George Illawarra, keeping him at the club as the head coach until the end of the [2021 NRL season](/wiki/2021_NRL_season "2021 NRL season") after the side had won four of their first six games.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

St. George Illawarra would then go to only win three of their next 17 matches which left them second last on the table above the last placed Gold Coast. On 2 September 2019, it was revealed that McGregor's position at head coach would be coming under review following the conclusion of the season. St. George Illawarra finished 15th on the ladder and did not qualify for the finals after having a horror season.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

On 13 August 2020, McGregor was terminated as head coach of St. George Illawarra after a poor start to the season left the club with four wins in thirteen games, he was subsequently replaced by [Dean Young](/wiki/Dean_Young_(rugby_league) "Dean Young (rugby league)").[[19]](#cite_note-19) At the end of the [2020 NRL season](/wiki/2020_NRL_season "2020 NRL season"), the club finished a disappointing 12th on the table and missed out on the finals.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Anthony Griffin (2021–2023)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Anthony Griffin (2021–2023)")]

On 7 September 2020, [Anthony Griffin](/wiki/Anthony_Griffin_(rugby_league) "Anthony Griffin (rugby league)") was appointed the coach for [2021 NRL season](/wiki/2021_NRL_season "2021 NRL season") on a two-year deal.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

On 1 June 2021, it was announced that [Jack de Belin](/wiki/Jack_de_Belin "Jack de Belin") would return from his 987-day hiatus after charges against him were dropped.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On 5 July 2021, the [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") handed out a total of $305,000 in fines to 13 players of the club after they attended [Paul Vaughan](/wiki/Paul_Vaughan_(rugby_league) "Paul Vaughan (rugby league)")'s property for a party in the midst of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") restrictions which were in breach of the code's bio security protocols.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The season finished with the club finishing in 11th place,[[24]](#cite_note-24) missing out on finals for the third consecutive year. The season started with four wins from the first five games, however over the next 19 games, the club only obtained another four victories, with the club losing eight matches in a row to close out the season after the infamous barbeque. It capped off a mediocre first year under Griffin's reign.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
St. George Illawarra finished the [2022 NRL season](/wiki/2022_NRL_season "2022 NRL season") in 10th place and missed the finals. Between round 17 to round 22, the club managed to win only one match which saw them go from 8th to 11th on the table. St. George Illawarra would win their last three matches of the season to finish in 10th position.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Before the start of the [2023 NRL season](/wiki/2023_NRL_season "2023 NRL season"), many experts and pundits predicted St. George Illawarra to finish with the [Wooden Spoon](/wiki/Wooden_Spoon_(award) "Wooden Spoon (award)"). After having a bye in the first round, the club defeated the Gold Coast in round 2 by a score of 32–18. In March 2023, it was revealed that Griffin had been told by club officials that they were looking for a potential coaching replacement in 2024 and that Griffin would have to re-apply for his position.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)
On 16 May 2023, it was announced that St. George Illawarra had terminated Griffin's contract effective immediately. Griffin was informed of the decision at 8AM just before the players training session.[[29]](#cite_note-GriffinSacked-29)
On 14 June 2023, it was announced that former [Cronulla](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") head coach [Shane Flanagan](/wiki/Shane_Flanagan "Shane Flanagan") had signed a three-year deal to be St. George Illawarra's new coach starting in 2024.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
In late June 2023, star player Ben Hunt requested an immediate release from his St. George Illawarra contract. It was reported that Hunt, became unhappy after head coach, Anthony Griffin, was sacked by the club. On 26 June, Hunt's request to be released by St. George Illawarra was denied by the club.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)
St. George Illawarra would finish the 2023 NRL season in 16th place, just two points above wooden spooners the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Shane Flanagan (2024-present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Shane Flanagan (2024-present)")]

Before the start of the [2024 NRL season](/wiki/2024_NRL_season "2024 NRL season"), many pundits predicted St. George Illawarra to finish with the [Wooden Spoon](/wiki/Wooden_Spoon_(award) "Wooden Spoon (award)") after the club had finished 16th the previous year. The club would surprise many however earning victories over Melbourne, Penrith and the New Zealand Warriors who had made the preliminary final in 2023. With two matches left, the club needed just one victory to secure a finals spot but lost both games and finished 11th on the table.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
In the [2025 NRL season](/wiki/2025_NRL_season "2025 NRL season"), St. George Illawarra endured a difficult campaign winning three of there first 7 fixtures and then had a horrid season, going on a 5 match losing streak under halfback [Kyle Flanagan](/wiki/Kyle_Flanagan_(rugby_league) "Kyle Flanagan (rugby league)"), St. George Illawarra, also suffered other embarrassing losses throughout the year including losing 40 nil to perennial rivals the [South Sydney Rabbitohs](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") and suffering a 56–6 loss against the [Dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins_(NRL) "Dolphins (NRL)"). The club would eventually finish 15th on the table. There was also talk throughout the year of disharmony and nepotism behind the scenes at the club. On 8 August 2025, Shane Flanagan's contract as head coach was extended until the end of 2028.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Colours and jerseys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Colours and jerseys")]

### Name and emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Name and emblem")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/82/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons_Secondary_Logo.svg/250px-St._George_Illawarra_Dragons_Secondary_Logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:St._George_Illawarra_Dragons_Secondary_Logo.svg)

Dragons secondary logo 2024-present

The team colours of the St. George Illawarra Dragons are red and white, colours shared by both the St. George Dragons and Illawarra Steelers. The merged entity's logo was taken directly from that of St George, with the addition of "Illawarra" to the bottom of the emblem. The emblem reflected the rich history of the St George Dragons, including an unprecedented and unmatched 11 consecutive premierships from 1956 to 1966, and the future strength of the club with the Illawarra.

The club name Dragons, is a shortened version of "Dragonslayers", the original nickname of St George. The emblem features a red shield with a silhouette of [St. George](/wiki/St._George "St. George"), overlaid with a white stylised [Dragon](/wiki/Dragon "Dragon"), with the words "ST. GEORGE" and "ILLAWARRA" above and below the shield respectively. It was originally intended for St George to face right and the Dragon to face left.

In October 2024, the Dragons unveiled a digital rebrand of the iconic primary logo, which has defined the St George Illawarra Dragons since the club's inception. The primary shield logo continues to feature prominently on club jerseys, ensuring that the heritage and essence of the club remain firmly rooted in their visual identity.[[37]](#cite_note-37) However, it is in the digital space where the new logo will take centre stage. This update aims to simplify the logo, while respecting and preserving the rich heritage of the Dragons. The dragon in the new secondary logo has been adapted directly from the one within the traditional shield, ensuring a respectful tribute to the past while meeting the demands of today's digital world.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Jerseys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Jerseys")]

When the St George Illawarra Dragons were formed in 1998, it was decided that the jersey of St George (first used in 1945), the famous "Red V" on white, would become the main jersey for the new club. The red used was changed to the scarlet tone used by the Steelers.

The alternate jersey initially used by the club was red and white horizontal stripes, similar to the 1921 St George Jersey known as the "blood and bandages". Since 2006, this Jersey has been reserved for use as the St George Illawarra Heritage Jersey for special occasions. This Jersey is worn during [Heritage Round](/wiki/NRL_Heritage_Round "NRL Heritage Round"), when the Dragons confront fierce local rivals the [Bulldogs RLFC](/wiki/Bulldogs_RLFC "Bulldogs RLFC").

In 2004 and 2005, the club used a jersey similar to the Steelers main jersey for Heritage matches, acknowledging the rugby league history of the Illawarra region, though not as a full-time alternate strip. This jersey was predominantly red with white stripes on the sleeves. With [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") producing the club's official merchandise from 2006, a generic Adidas design – predominantly red with three white stripes down the sleeves – was adopted as the full-time alternate strip; before another re-design in 2008, which better reflected the old Steelers jersey.

Reebok produced supporter and team kits from the 2010 season until 2013. Again the Red V was the Main jersey and the Alternate jersey was a red design with white slash marks. [ISC](/wiki/International_Sports_Clothing "International Sports Clothing") became St George Illawarra's apparel partner in 2014 until 2017. [XBlades](/wiki/XBlades "XBlades") became the main supplier for the 2018 season and ended their contract in 2020. [Classic Sportswear](/wiki/Classic_Sportswear "Classic Sportswear") currently produce the club's team kits and supporter gear.

## Home grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Home grounds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/OKI_Jubilee_Grandstand.jpg/250px-OKI_Jubilee_Grandstand.jpg)](/wiki/File:OKI_Jubilee_Grandstand.jpg)

Inside Jubilee Oval

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/WIN_Stadium5.jpg/250px-WIN_Stadium5.jpg)](/wiki/File:WIN_Stadium5.jpg)

Inside WIN Stadium

The Dragons have two home grounds, reflecting the joint nature of the club:

* [WIN Stadium](/wiki/WIN_Stadium "WIN Stadium") (1999–present)
* [Jubilee Oval](/wiki/Jubilee_Oval "Jubilee Oval") (1999, 2003–07, 2009–present)

Half of the Dragons home matches are played at [WIN Stadium](/wiki/WIN_Stadium "WIN Stadium") (previously the home ground of the Illawarra Steelers) and the half at [Jubilee Oval](/wiki/Jubilee_Oval "Jubilee Oval") (previously the home of the St George Dragons).

The club has previously played Sydney home games out of the [Sydney Football Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(1988) "Sydney Football Stadium (1988)") (2000–2002) and [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia") (2008), as well as the [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground") on special occasions.

The club has taken their home ground advantage away from their usual home grounds to the [Sydney Football Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(1988) "Sydney Football Stadium (1988)") for the [Club ANZAC Game](/wiki/Club_ANZAC_Game "Club ANZAC Game") against the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") in the 2004, 2006, 2010, 2012, 2014, 2016 and 2018 seasons. In 2002 when the match was first held, the Sydney Football Stadium was, at the time, one of the Dragons' main home grounds, whilst in 2008 the match was played at ANZ Stadium, a very unpopular move amongst supporters.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/CarltonStadium.jpg/250px-CarltonStadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:CarltonStadium.jpg)

Outside Jubilee Oval, that includes a Walk of Fame

### Right Game Right Venue Strategy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Right Game Right Venue Strategy")]

In 2013, the club entered into a four-year agreement with the NRL to move 4 of their 12 home games to one of the major Sydney stadiums (Allianz Stadium, Sydney Cricket Ground, ANZ Stadium).

The strategic intent was to replicate the success of the annual game played against [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") on ANZAC day. As part of this initiative, the club and the NRL sought to introduce an annual Heritage Round game against South Sydney at the [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground").

In 2017, the club announced that the NRL had only provided three appropriate games to support this initiative for the 2017 season.

It was announced that from the [2018 NRL season](/wiki/2018_NRL_season "2018 NRL season") that all games (except for the ANZAC clash against the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") and one other round) would be shared equally between Kogarah and Wollongong.

## Leagues clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Leagues clubs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/StGeorgeL1.JPG/250px-StGeorgeL1.JPG)](/wiki/File:StGeorgeL1.JPG)

St George Leagues Club, Beverley Park, Sydney

As well as having two administrative offices, St. George Illawarra are supported by two separate Leagues clubs – one in each of the St. George and Illawarra areas.

### [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: St. George Dragons")]

The St. George Leagues Club is located on the [Princes Highway](/wiki/Princes_Highway "Princes Highway") at [Beverley Park](/wiki/Beverley_Park,_New_South_Wales "Beverley Park, New South Wales") close to the northern home ground of [Jubilee Oval](/wiki/OKI_Jubilee_Stadium "OKI Jubilee Stadium") at [Carlton](/wiki/Carlton,_New_South_Wales "Carlton, New South Wales"). Established in 1963, St. George was one of the first Super Leagues clubs developed in the 60s and was commonly referred to as the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal") because of the use of white marble in the original building.[[39]](#cite_note-39)
Very little of the original building is still there today after extensive refurbishing and redesigning the entire club to make it one of the most superbly fitting clubs in Australia.

### [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Illawarra Steelers")]

Situated in the middle of the City Beach precinct, the Steelers Club is located adjacent to WIN Entertainment Centre and [WIN Stadium](/wiki/WIN_Stadium "WIN Stadium"). It is directly across the road from the grounds Western Grandstand. Established in 1990, the club has struggled financially against much larger and more popular leagues clubs in Wollongong, such as Collegians, Dapto Leagues Club, Wests Illawarra Leagues Club, and Shellharbour Workers Club in Shellharbour. However, after a major restructure of its operations, the Steelers Club traded profitably. Twenty percent of the club premises were sold to Bermuda-based Billionaire and owner of WIN Corp, [Bruce Gordon](/wiki/Bruce_Gordon_(businessman) "Bruce Gordon (businessman)"). The sale fetched $2.6 million.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Supporters")]

Many supporters of St. George Illawarra mainly come from the club's local areas, the suburbs of the St. George district in Sydney (the Bayside and Georges River Council regions), and the Illawarra on the south-coast of NSW. The St. George Illawarra Dragons also have a huge following in south-east Queensland, as a significant number of the club's 'Red V' memberships are from people in this area. The St. George Illawarra club also have a large number of supporters from all over New South Wales. At the end of the [2023 NRL season](/wiki/2023_NRL_season "2023 NRL season"), the club recorded 19,965 members which was significantly higher than both Manly and Cronulla but lower than the remaining six Sydney teams.[[42]](#cite_note-42)
A fan run podcast, called the Red V Podcast was launched in January 2020. Discussing the latest news, match previews and reviews, talking with current and former players and giving fans a voice. The podcast is hosted by lifelong Dragons fan Jack Clifton and Curtis Woodward.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Due to St. George enjoying great success over the years, St. George Illawarra have inherited several fierce rivalries, while also creating a few new ones since forming the joint venture.

### Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs")]

St. George Illawarra has a fierce rivalry with neighbour the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs"). Canterbury-Bankstown were founded in 1935, 14 years after St. George. St. George inflicted a RL record 91–6 defeat of Canterbury in 1935[[50]](#cite_note-rl1908_bulldogs-50) but Canterbury enjoyed premiership success first, however St. George recorded 11 straight premierships in the years following (1956–1966).

In the [1942 NSWRFL season](/wiki/1942_NSWRFL_season "1942 NSWRFL season"), the two clubs met in the 1942 grand final with Canterbury-Bankstown defeating St. George 11–9 in a low-scoring affair at the [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground").[[50]](#cite_note-rl1908_bulldogs-50)

It was also Canterbury-Bankstown who put an end to their Premiership run in 1967, when Canterbury beat them by one point in the preliminary final to face South Sydney in the Grand Final. Since then, both clubs have inflicted Premiership defeats on the other, St George defeating Canterbury-Bankstown in 1979, Canterbury returning the favour in 1985.[[50]](#cite_note-rl1908_bulldogs-50)

The two teams subsequently met in the 1993 preliminary final which St. George won 27–12. They would meet again in the 1995 and 1998 finals series with Canterbury running out winners on both occasions. The elimination final in 1998 was also St. George's final game as a stand-alone entity as the club elected to form a joint venture with [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") for the [1999 NRL season](/wiki/1999_NRL_season "1999 NRL season").

Since St. George formed a joint venture with Illawarra, the two clubs have met each other in the 2001 finals series which St. George Illawarra defeated Canterbury 23-22 and in the 2015 finals series which Canterbury won 11–10 at [ANZ Stadium](/wiki/ANZ_Stadium "ANZ Stadium"). In the [2017 NRL season](/wiki/2017_NRL_season "2017 NRL season"), St. George Illawarra needed to beat Canterbury in the final game of the regular season to qualify for the finals. Canterbury who were already out of contention for the finals defeated St. George 26–20 which allowed [North Queensland](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") to get the last finals spot and finish 8th. North Queensland would then go on to reach the [2017 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2017_NRL_Grand_Final "2017 NRL Grand Final") as a result.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-rl1908_bulldogs-50)

The two sides are rivals in the [NRL Heritage Round](/wiki/NRL_Heritage_Round "NRL Heritage Round"). Canterbury and St. George Illawarra also unofficially have a territorial rivalry, with fans from both teams coming in large amounts around [Arncliffe](/wiki/Arncliffe,_New_South_Wales "Arncliffe, New South Wales"), [Earlwood](/wiki/Earlwood "Earlwood"), [Kingsgrove](/wiki/Kingsgrove,_New_South_Wales "Kingsgrove, New South Wales"), [Marrickville](/wiki/Marrickville,_New_South_Wales "Marrickville, New South Wales"), and [Panania](/wiki/Panania "Panania").

### Parramatta Eels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Parramatta Eels")]

The St. George Illawarra Dragons and [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") had a competitive rivalry that stretched back to 1977, when the St. George Dragons and Parramatta played out the first ever drawn Grand Final result. Parramatta, seeking their first minor premiership after they finished the season on the top of the ladder at the end of the regular season, were beaten 22–0 in the Grand Final Rematch by St. George, who won their first Grand Final since 1966.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

There were many controversial matches between St. George Illawarra and Parramatta. Firstly, in round 18 of the [2005 NRL season](/wiki/2005_NRL_season "2005 NRL season"), Parramatta won 40–14 in a match which saw [Trent Barrett](/wiki/Trent_Barrett "Trent Barrett") and [PJ Marsh](/wiki/PJ_Marsh "PJ Marsh") trade blows after Marsh's crude charge-down attempt at Barrett, sparking an all-in brawl whilst Parramatta's [Wade McKinnon](/wiki/Wade_McKinnon "Wade McKinnon") sprinted to score a match-turning 80 metre try.

A less memorable match saw no points scored between the two teams in round 13 2006 in the first 70 minutes of play before Parramatta slotted a field goal with nine minutes remaining, before St. George Illawarra struck back with two one-pointers to take a 2–1 lead. St. George Illawarra then scored a try through [Matt Cooper](/wiki/Matt_Cooper_(rugby_league) "Matt Cooper (rugby league)") with mere seconds remaining to claim an 8–1 victory.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In the 2009 NRL finals series, St. George Illawarra finished as the [Minor Premiership](/wiki/Minor_Premiership "Minor Premiership") and faced the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") who finished 8th on the ladder. A week earlier at the same venue, St. George Illawarra had defeated Parramatta 37–0 but in the finals game, Parramatta won 25–12 with Parramatta player [Jarryd Hayne](/wiki/Jarryd_Hayne "Jarryd Hayne") scoring a brilliant solo try. As of the [2023 NRL season](/wiki/2023_NRL_season "2023 NRL season"), this has been the last time where St. George Illawarra and Parramatta have met in a finals series.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks")]

St. George Illawarra's fiercest rivalries is with their Southern neighbor, [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks"). Cronulla-Sutherland originally were part of the St. George juniors area, and Cronulla are often referred to as St George's "little brother".[[55]](#cite_note-55)
In Cronulla's foundation year, the club was coached by [Ken Kearney](/wiki/Ken_Kearney "Ken Kearney") who won several premierships with St. George as a player-coach in the 1950s and early 1960s. Cronulla's inaugural captain was also a former St. George premiership winning player in [Monty Porter](/wiki/Monty_Porter "Monty Porter"). In 1978, former St. George player and rugby league immortal [Norm Provan](/wiki/Norm_Provan "Norm Provan") coached Cronulla to the grand final and replay that season against Manly.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

St. George Illawarra have had the same amount of success as the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks"), who have also won one premiership. In fact, since St. George Illawarra entered the competition in 1999, both the Saints and Cronulla have finished higher than the other six times apiece.

Cronulla finished the [1999 season](/wiki/1999_NRL_season "1999 NRL season") as minor premiers, but St. George Illawarra beat them 26–8 in the preliminary final to progress to the 1999 NRL Grand Final at the Sharks' expense. The Saints trailed 0–8 at halftime.

Both clubs have been accused of poaching players from the other, and compete for fans in the same region. In 1999, the Cronulla CEO infamously cut up a St. George Jersey after a match at [Toyota Stadium](/wiki/Endeavour_Field "Endeavour Field"). During the Super League war, Cronulla-Sutherland was one of only three Sydney teams to join the rival competition, entrenching further spite from both the [St. George Dragons](/wiki/St._George_Dragons "St. George Dragons") and the [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") were loyal to the ARL during the [Super League War](/wiki/Super_League_War "Super League War").

In the 2002 finals series, both teams met in the semi-final which Cronulla won 40–24 at the [Sydney Football Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(1988) "Sydney Football Stadium (1988)"). The rivalry increased further in 2005 when they met in a qualifying final at a sold out [WIN Stadium](/wiki/WIN_Stadium "WIN Stadium") with the Dragons winning 28–22. As of the [2022 NRL season](/wiki/2022_NRL_season "2022 NRL season"), this is the last time the two club's have played each other in a finals game.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In 2009, former Dragons captain [Trent Barrett](/wiki/Trent_Barrett "Trent Barrett") switched to Cronulla-Sutherland after two years in the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"). In his return match to Kogarah in round 3, 2009, Barrett was injured and with him went any hopes Cronulla had of winning the premiership (in the preceding year, the Cronulla club had finished equal-first) that year. Other players to have switched clubs include Lance Thompson and Jeremy Smith (St. George Illawarra to Cronulla), Beau Scott, Sam Isemonger and Matt Bickerstaff (Cronulla to St. George Illawarra) and Colin Best (Cronulla to St. George Illawarra) and then back again, as of 2011. In another match in 2009, Cronulla captain [Paul Gallen](/wiki/Paul_Gallen "Paul Gallen") was found guilty of a racial slur involving then Dragons forward [Mickey Paea](/wiki/Mickey_Paea "Mickey Paea"). In round 18 of the [2023 NRL season](/wiki/2023_NRL_season "2023 NRL season"), St. George Illawarra recorded their worst ever loss to Cronulla as either St. George or St. George Illawarra with the match finishing 52–16.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### South Sydney Rabbitohs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: South Sydney Rabbitohs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/4/43/Charity_Shield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charity_Shield.jpg)

[Charity Shield](/wiki/Rugby_League_Charity_Shield_(Australia) "Rugby League Charity Shield (Australia)") logo

St. George won 15 premierships before forming a joint-venture with Illawarra. In 2010, they won their first NRL premiership as a joint-venture. [South Sydney](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") have won the most premierships with 21, the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") next on 15 premierships. South Sydney and St. George have met several times in grand finals prior to the joint-venture and being the north-eastern neighbours of St. George, had many fierce encounters. One of the biggest matches in this rivalry is the [Charity Shield](/wiki/Rugby_League_Charity_Shield_(Australia) "Rugby League Charity Shield (Australia)"), a pre-season fixture between the two sides that was abandoned after the 1999 match but was revived after Souths were re-admitted to the NRL in 2002.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In 2001, South Sydney chairman and club legend [George Piggins](/wiki/George_Piggins "George Piggins") said there would be no chance of the Charity Shield being revived if Souths were to be included back into the [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") saying "The Dragons: They sold us out". This was in reference to St. George signing an affidavit at the time which included that it would be detrimental if Souths were returned to the competition.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In 2018, both sides met for the first time in a finals match since 1984. After 80 tense minutes of absorbing action, Souths won a close semi-final 13–12.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Sydney Roosters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Sydney Roosters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Dragons_vs_Roosters%2C_Anzac_Day_2018.jpg/250px-Dragons_vs_Roosters%2C_Anzac_Day_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dragons_vs_Roosters,_Anzac_Day_2018.jpg)

The pre-match formalities taking place prior to the 2018 Anzac Day clash between the two clubs.

St. George has another fierce rivalry with the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"). St. George's first premiership was a 31–14 victory over Easts in the [1941 NSWRFL season](/wiki/1941_NSWRFL_season "1941 NSWRFL season") Grand Final.

In 1960 Eastern Suburbs reached their first grand final since 1945 where they faced St. George but were beaten 31–6.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In 1975 Eastern Suburbs defeated St. George 38–0 in the grand final which until the [2008 NRL season](/wiki/2008_NRL_season "2008 NRL season") was the biggest winning margin in a grand final.

During 1995, in the midst of the [Super League War](/wiki/Super_League_War "Super League War"), Eastern Suburbs had secret discussions to merge with the St. George club, with St. George's jersey and emblem to disappear. Fans of both clubs were outraged, and the attempt aborted. St. George Illawarra annually contests the [Club ANZAC Game](/wiki/Club_ANZAC_Game "Club ANZAC Game") against the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"), a tradition dating back to the 1970s, and contested annually since 2002.

After St. George formed a joint venture with the [Illawarra Steelers](/wiki/Illawarra_Steelers "Illawarra Steelers") in 1999 to form [St. George Illawarra](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra "St. George Illawarra") the two clubs have met each other twice in finals matches, the first coming in 1999 which St. George Illawarra won and in the [2010 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2010_NRL_Grand_Final "2010 NRL Grand Final") where St. George Illawarra won its first premiership as a joint venture defeating the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") 32–8 at [ANZ Stadium](/wiki/ANZ_Stadium "ANZ Stadium").[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

## Encounters with other teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Encounters with other teams")]

### Brisbane Broncos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Brisbane Broncos")]

Prior to the joint-venture, both St George and Illawarra had memorable matches with Brisbane in the early 1990s. St. George and [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") contested the [1992](/wiki/1992_NSWRL_season "1992 NSWRL season") and [1993](/wiki/1993_NSWRL_season "1993 NSWRL season") [Grand Finals](/wiki/Grand_final "Grand final"), creating a competitive rivalry (see [New South Wales Rugby League season 1993](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_season_1993 "New South Wales Rugby League season 1993")). Also, Illawarra had produced some memorable performances against Brisbane, including the infamous [1989 Panasonic Cup](/wiki/1989_Panasonic_Cup_(rugby_league) "1989 Panasonic Cup (rugby league)") Final (which Brisbane won) and the 1992 Tooheys Challenge Cup Final (won by Illawarra).

The St. George Illawarra Dragons had the longest winning streak against the Brisbane Broncos than any other club (eight, from round 23, 2005 to round 4, 2009 inclusive, including one final in 2006), across five years. With former [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") coach [Wayne Bennett](/wiki/Wayne_Bennett_(rugby_league) "Wayne Bennett (rugby league)") at the helm of St George Illawarra, and [Wendell Sailor](/wiki/Wendell_Sailor "Wendell Sailor"), a popular face in the NRL and a former Bronco, the rivalry only increased. In 2009, the Dragons defeated the Broncos at [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Suncorp_Stadium "Suncorp Stadium") early, and were then beaten in [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong") by the Broncos later on in the season. One game a piece, the two sides met up in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") in the finals, where they lost to the Broncos 24–10, thus ending the Dragons season and also that of [Wendell Sailor](/wiki/Wendell_Sailor "Wendell Sailor")'s career. Ironically, his first and last games for the Dragons were both against the Brisbane side.

Some memorable matches between the Dragons and Brisbane have included:

* Round 11, 2002: Brisbane were at the top of the ladder undefeated at the time until they ran into the Dragons at [Aussie Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(1988) "Sydney Football Stadium (1988)") on a sunny Sunday afternoon. The Dragons won 28–20 to inflict Brisbane's first defeat of the season, but in round 17 the Broncos got them back with a 34–22 victory at [ANZ Stadium](/wiki/Queensland_Sport_and_Athletics_Centre "Queensland Sport and Athletics Centre").
* Round 26, 2003: St. George Illawarra were out of finals contention and the Broncos the previous week had lost a then club-record breaking sixth straight loss when the two teams came together at [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") in the final round of the 2003 season. The second-string Dragons side, without more than half of its first grade side stunned the Broncos 26–25 with hooker [Mark Riddell](/wiki/Mark_Riddell "Mark Riddell") kicking an improbable penalty goal with just over 90 seconds remaining. This condemned Brisbane to their seventh loss in succession and gave Dragons fans some hope for the 2004 season.
* Round 12, 2004: Not even an early try from [Mark Gasnier](/wiki/Mark_Gasnier "Mark Gasnier") (who was dropped from the [New South Wales State of Origin team](/wiki/New_South_Wales_rugby_league_team "New South Wales rugby league team") for disciplinary issues) could inspire St. George Illawarra as they lost by just two points, 24–22.
* Round 23, 2005: In a potential Grand Final preview, scores were locked at 0–0 for the majority of the first half before the Dragons broke away in the second half to record a 24–4 win and instigate Brisbane's then-traditional late-season poor form.
* Round 15, 2006: With the Dragons trailing 16–12 with two minutes remaining, Saints winger [Brett Morris](/wiki/Brett_Morris "Brett Morris") scored his first-ever first grade try in the corner in the dying minutes after catching a [Mathew Head](/wiki/Mathew_Head "Mathew Head") [bomb](/wiki/Bomb_(kick) "Bomb (kick)"). The try was allowed, leaving St. George Illawarra hooker [Aaron Gorrell](/wiki/Aaron_Gorrell "Aaron Gorrell") (in a twist of irony, he later went on to play for the Broncos) with the sideline conversion to win the game. Gorrell nailed it and Saints won the match 18–16.
* 2nd Qualifying Final, 2006: The Red V upset Brisbane 20–4, the third time St. George Illawarra defeated Brisbane in that year. Brisbane ultimately went on to win the premiership.
* Round 13, 2008: [Wendell Sailor](/wiki/Wendell_Sailor "Wendell Sailor") played his first game for the Dragons against the Brisbane club, but was rested just before halftime following a jaw injury suffered in the first half. Saints won the game 34–16.
* Round 23, 2008: Sailor marked his return to [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") with his first try since coming back to rugby league in a win to St. George Illawarra, 24–20.
* Round 4, 2009: [Wayne Bennett](/wiki/Wayne_Bennett_(rugby_league) "Wayne Bennett (rugby league)"), [Darius Boyd](/wiki/Darius_Boyd "Darius Boyd") and a host of others opposed the Broncos for the first time in their lives. Despite conceding an early try to [Israel Folau](/wiki/Israel_Folau "Israel Folau"), the Dragons won 25–12 with Wendell Sailor appropriately scoring the match winning try.
* 2nd Semi-Final, 2009: The Brisbane club ended St. George Illawarra's season with a 24–10 win, and the Dragons became the first minor premiers in 16 years to be eliminated in just the 2nd round of the Finals.
* 2nd Semi-Final, 2011: Brisbane beat St. George Illawarra 13–12 field goal kicked by Darren Lockyer in overtime, Darren won the game with a fractured cheekbone that he received late in the game, this also turned out to be Darren Lockyer's last game for the Broncos and Wayne Bennett (coaching) and Mark Gasnier's last for the Dragons.
* Round 1, 2018: In the season opener, [Ben Hunt](/wiki/Ben_Hunt_(rugby_league) "Ben Hunt (rugby league)") played his first game for St. George Illawarra after leaving Brisbane the year before, the Red V beat Brisbane 34–12 with Hunt scoring an intercept try off a poor pass from Broncos recruit [Matt Lodge](/wiki/Matt_Lodge "Matt Lodge").
* 2nd Elimination Final, 2018: In their first finals meeting in seven years, Brisbane were widely tipped to beat St. George Illawarra, after an arm wrestle for most of the 1st half, the Dragons inspired by a [Tariq Sims](/wiki/Tariq_Sims "Tariq Sims") hat trick went on to hand the Broncos their worst finals loss at Suncorp winning 48–18 and eliminating Brisbane in the process. It was also Ben Hunt's first game in Brisbane since leaving Brisbane for St George Illawarra.
* Round 3, 2019: In their only meeting for 2019, both sides struggled to get the upper hand until former Brisbane player [Corey Norman](/wiki/Corey_Norman "Corey Norman") slotted a field goal right on full time to give the Red V a 25–24 win after [Jamayne Isaako](/wiki/Jamayne_Isaako "Jamayne Isaako") missed a field goal attempt only moments earlier.
* Round 25, 2022: St. George Illawarra were out of finals contention while Brisbane had to win to play finals. The Red V won the game 22–12 condemning the Brisbane club to a third consecutive year without a finals appearance which allowed the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") to advance into the finals at the expense of Brisbane.

Players who played for the Dragons who have won premierships with Brisbane include [Darius Boyd](/wiki/Darius_Boyd "Darius Boyd") and [Luke Priddis](/wiki/Luke_Priddis "Luke Priddis"), who also won a premiership with [Penrith](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") in 2003. Others of note include [Nick Emmett](/wiki/Nick_Emmett "Nick Emmett") who was brought to the club by Wayne Bennett, and [Neville Costigan](/wiki/Neville_Costigan "Neville Costigan"), who was sacked just before the Broncos' 2006 success.

### Melbourne Storm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Melbourne Storm")]

These two teams played against each other in the [1999 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/1999_NRL_Grand_Final "1999 NRL Grand Final"). It is remembered for the penalty try conceded by St. George Illawarra winger [Jamie Ainscough](/wiki/Jamie_Ainscough "Jamie Ainscough") that cost the club the premiership. It was the club's first NRL Grand Final appearance as a joint-venture and Melbourne's first Grand Final appearance in just its second year in existence. St. George Illawarra had led 14–0 at halftime only for Melbourne to come back and claim a controversial 20–18 win following the penalty try two minutes from time.

The two teams have also had some memorable, not to mention controversial matches in the decade that followed, none more so than the Grand Final rematch in 2000 which saw Melbourne beat St. George Illawarra 70–10 at the [MCG](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground") (this remains St George Illawarra's biggest defeat as a joint-venture to date).

In the lead up to that match, then-Saints five-eighth [Anthony Mundine](/wiki/Anthony_Mundine "Anthony Mundine") had claimed that the Melbourne club were not worthy premiers, sparking anger from Melbourne's fans and players, thus the catalyst for the huge win. St. George Illawarra however did claim revenge later that year with a 50–4 win at WIN Stadium (that also remains Melbourne's worst ever defeat to date).[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The 2009 season opened with these two teams going head-to-head in another much talked about match. St. George Illawarra five-eighth [Jamie Soward](/wiki/Jamie_Soward "Jamie Soward") was labelled a 'rag doll' in defence whilst trying to stop Melbourne's [Greg Inglis](/wiki/Greg_Inglis "Greg Inglis") scoring a try, however ever since that match Soward's game has matured. The club lost 17–16 in extra time, with Inglis scoring the match winning field goal. In the return match, Soward was also the centre of attention as he used his foot to prevent Inglis again from scoring, resulting in an eight-point try and Soward being put on report (he was reprimanded for this). St. George Illawarra won this match 26–12. In round 4, 2010, St. George Illawarra were the last team to lose premiership points to Melbourne before their salary cap scandal was revealed.

St. George Illawarra were also involved in a controversial loss to Melbourne at the start of the 2014 season. In their round 6 game at [AAMI Park](/wiki/Melbourne_Rectangular_Stadium "Melbourne Rectangular Stadium"), a refereeing and timekeeping error resulted in Melbourne scoring a try after the siren had gone, to win them the match. In the 80th minute, St. George Illawarra were ahead 24–22. The full-time siren sounded in the 80th minute, prior to a Melbourne player in possession being tackled. After the tackle was completed, the referees allowed play to continue, rather than ending the match, which gave Melbourne the chance to spread the ball in an elongated play, which resulted in a try and conversion in the 81st minute, giving them the win, 28–24.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In their only meeting in the 2024 season, St. George Illawarra defeated Melbourne as the away team for the first time since the [1999 Qualifying Final](/wiki/1999_NRL_season#Finals "1999 NRL season"), breaking a 25-year losing streak over 17 matches in Victoria.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

## Women's team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._George_Illawarra_Dragons&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Women's team")]

Main article: [St. George Illawarra Dragons Women](/wiki/St._George_Illawarra_Dragons_Women "St. George Illawarra Dragons Women")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c0/Dragons_NRL_Women%27s_Premiership_players.jpg/250px-Dragons_NRL_Women%27s_Premiership_players.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dragons_NRL_Women%27s_Premiership_players.jpg)

Members of the NRL Women's team assembling outside [Jubilee Oval](/wiki/Jubilee_Oval "Jubilee Oval") during a promotional appearance in August 2018.

In December 2017, the St. George Illawarra Dragons expressed their interest in applying for a licence to participate in the inaugural [NRL Women's](/wiki/NRL_Women%27s "NRL Women's") season.[[70]](#cite_note-70) In March 2018, they were awarded one of four licences for the league's [inaugural season](/wiki/2018_NRL_Women%27s_season "2018 NRL Women's season"), to commence in September of the same year.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Daniel Lacey was appointed to coach the side.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

In June 2018, [Sam Bremner](/wiki/Sam_Bremner "Sam Bremner"), [Kezie Apps](/wiki/Kezie_Apps "Kezie Apps") and [Talesha Quinn](/wiki/Talesha_Quinn "Talesha Quinn") were unveiled as the club's first three signings.[[73]](#cite_note-73)
